> 6. **Drawing arrows on diagrams that point the wrong way.** In flexion diagrams, the arrow must show **decreasing angle**.
> 7. **Forgetting FM & CM multipliers in RWL.** Omitting these leads to an inflated RWL and a falsely "safe" Lifting Index.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **Three anatomical planes:** Sagittal (flexion/extension), Frontal (abduction/adduction), Transverse (rotation).
- ☐ **Six fundamental joint movements:** Flexion, Extension, Abduction, Adduction, Rotation, Circumduction.
- ☐ **Static work = isometric; Dynamic work = isotonic.**
- ☐ **Static work is ~4× more fatiguing** due to blood-flow occlusion.
- ☐ **Karvonen Formula:** $HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + (HRR \times \%\,intensity)$.
- ☐ **$HR_{max}$ formula:** 220 − Age.
- ☐ **Harris-Benedict BMR (Men):** 88.362 + (13.397 × W) + (4.799 × H) − (5.677 × A).
- ☐ **NIOSH RWL:** $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$.
- ☐ **Lifting Index (LI):** $LI = L / RWL$. **LI > 1 = hazardous**.
- ☐ **Continuous 8-hr shift:** Working HR should not exceed **110 bpm**; HRR should stay **below 50%**.
- ☐ **Mechanical Work:** $W = m \times g \times h$ (in Joules); convert to kcal by dividing by 4184.
- ☐ **Energy demand increases** from sitting → standing → walking → lifting → heavy manual labour.
- ☐ **Posture analysis tools:** RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment), REBA (Rapid Entire Body Assessment).
- ☐ **Always include units** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners award partial marks for correct units alone.
- ☐ **Squat lift > Stoop lift** for load-bearing tasks because leg muscles are stronger than back muscles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstration of Air Sampling Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL607 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND INDUSTRIAL HYGIENE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of Air Sampling Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Sampling** is the scientific process of capturing a representative volume of air from a specific environment (ambient atmosphere, workplace, or emission source) using calibrated mechanical devices, in order to qualitatively identify and/or quantitatively measure the concentration of airborne contaminants such as **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **gaseous pollutants**, **vapours**, **bioaerosols**, and **fumes**.

In the context of **Industrial Hygiene**, air sampling is classified into four primary domains:

| Domain | Purpose | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Ambient Air Sampling** | Determine background pollutant levels in the outdoor environment | NAAQS compliance (CPCB monitoring stations) |
| **Stack Sampling** | Measure emissions from industrial flue gases | Regulatory compliance, EP Act 1986 |
| **Personal Sampling** | Assess worker exposure in the breathing zone | OSHA/PEL compliance, IH exposure assessment |
| **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Sampling** | Evaluate air inside buildings, offices, hospitals | ASHRAE Standard 62.1, sick building syndrome |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The objective of Module Experiment 2 is to *demonstrate the working principle, components, and handling procedure* of standard air sampling instruments. Students must identify each part and explain its function in the viva-voce.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a doctor drawing a few millilitres of blood from a patient to diagnose what is circulating inside the entire body.

- The **blood sample** = the captured air volume
- The **needle + syringe** = the sampling pump and filter media
- The **pathology lab** = the analytical balance / spectrophotometer
- The **diagnosis report** = the pollutant concentration (mg/m³ or ppm)

In the same way, a tiny "lungful" of polluted air is drawn through a filter, weighed, and the contaminant concentration is *extrapolated* back to the total air mass the worker or the environment was exposed to. This is the foundation of every air monitoring study in environmental engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Air sampling is NOT about collecting all the air — it is about drawing a *kinematically and chemically representative* fraction. If the sample is biased (e.g., non-isokinetic in a stack, or breathing-zone distance too far in personal sampling), the entire result is invalid.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **NAAQS (India, 2009):** PM₁₀ = **60 µg/m³** (24-hr), PM₂.₅ = **40 µg/m³** (24-hr)
- **CPCB Tolerance Limits (Stack):** Particulate Matter ≤ **150 mg/Nm³** (for cement plants)
- **Workplace Exposure Limits:** OSHA PEL, ACGIH TLV
- **Universal Flow Reference:** **1 m³ = 1000 L**, **1 ppm (gas) ≈ (MW/24.45) mg/m³** at **25 °C and 1 atm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Air Sampling Equipment

Air sampling instruments are classified by the **phase** of the contaminant they collect:

### A. Particulate (Aerosol) Samplers

1. **High Volume Sampler (HVS / Hi-Vol)**
   - Captures **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)** on a glass-fibre filter
   - Flow rate: **1.1 to 1.7 m³/min** (≈ 60–100 cfm)
   - Used for ambient air and stationary sources
   - Operates on the principle of **blower suction through a filter paper**

2. **Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)**
   - Uses a **cyclone separator** to remove non-respirable fraction (>10 µm)
   - Collects only **PM₁₀** (or PM₂.₅ with finer cyclones)
   - Flow rate: **1.0 to 1.5 m³/min** (BGI, Casella models)

3. **Personal Cascade Impactor / PEM (Personal Environmental Monitor)**
   - Worn in the breathing zone (within **30 cm of nose & mouth**)
   - Operates on **impaction** principle using successive stages
   - Battery-powered diaphragm pump, low flow (**2 to 4 L/min**)

4. **Impactor (Anderson, Hirst)**
   - Multi-stage jet impactor, classifies particles by **aerodynamic diameter**
   - Stages collect progressively finer particles

5. **Impinger / Fritted Bubbler**
   - For **gaseous pollutants (SO₂, NO₂, NH₃, HCHO)**
   - Air is bubbled through an absorbing solution (e.g., **Sodium Tetrachloromercurate for SO₂** — West-Gaeke method)

### B. Gas/Vapour Samplers

1. **Detector Tubes (Dräger, Gastec, Kitagawa)**
   - Colorimetric length-of-stain measurement
   - Single-shot, instantaneous readout
   - Range: ppb to % levels

2. **Tedlar / Canister Sampling Bags**
   - For VOCs (BTEX), later analysed by GC-MS

3. **Activated Charcoal / Silica Gel Tubes**
   - Passive (diffusive) or active pumping
   - Desorbed with **CS₂** and analysed by GC-FID

### C. Stack Sampling Train (Isokinetic)

The standard **EPA Method 5 / IS 11255** train includes:
- Nozzle → Pitot tube → Probe → Filter holder (heated) → Impingers (4 nos.) → Condenser → Pump → Dry gas meter → Orifice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Trick Question:** *"Why is the filter in Method 5 maintained at 120 °C?"* — To prevent water vapour condensation that would artificially increase the particulate mass (gravimetric bias).

## 2.2 The 'Why & How' Behind Each Component

| Component | Why it Exists | How it Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Blower / Vacuum Pump** | Create the pressure differential | Rotating impeller generates suction of 1.0–1.7 m³/min |
| **Filter Media (Glass Fibre / Quartz)** | Trap particulates | Inert, high collection efficiency (>99% for 0.3 µm DOP) |
| **Rotameter / Mass Flow Controller** | Maintain constant flow despite filter loading | Float in tapered tube; orifice meters use ΔP |
| **Cyclone** | Fractionate by size | Centrifugal force throws large particles to wall → hopper |
| **Impinger (with absorbing reagent)** | Dissolve gases into liquid for analysis | Gas bubbles through fine frit → mass transfer (Ficks law) |
| **Dry Gas Meter (DGM)** | Record cumulative volume at line conditions | Bellows expand/contract with each pulse |
| **Pitot Tube (Stack)** | Measure stack gas velocity | ΔP between stagnation and static taps → Bernoulli |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampled Volume | $V = Q \times t$ | m³ | Q in m³/min, t in min |
| 2 | Particulate Concentration | $C = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i)}{V} \times 10^6$ | µg/m³ | W in grams, V in m³ |
| 3 | Standardised Volume (STP) | $V_s = V \times \dfrac{P \times 298}{760 \times (T+273)}$ | Nm³ | For DGM reading correction |
| 4 | ppm to mg/m³ (Gas) | $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ | mg/m³ | 24.45 = molar volume at 25 °C, 1 atm |
| 5 | mg/m³ to ppm (Gas) | $C_{ppm} = C_{mg/m^3} \times \dfrac{24.45}{MW}$ | ppm | Invert conversion |
| 6 | Stack Gas Velocity | $v = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta H}{\rho_g}}$ | m/s | Pitot, Cp ≈ 0.84–0.99 |
| 7 | Isokinetic Ratio | $I = \dfrac{Q_{sampler}}{Q_{stack}} \times \dfrac{D_n^2}{D_s^2}$ | — | Must be 0.95–1.05 |
| 8 | Cyclone 50% Cut-Point (d₅₀) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu W}{2 \pi N_c \rho_p (N_s - 1)}}$ | µm | Stairmand equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Always carry the **factor of 10⁶** when converting grams of dust to micrograms. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for unit conversion in a 14-mark problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Occupational Health:** Quantifying a textile worker's exposure to cotton dust (byssinosis prevention)
- **Regulatory Permitting:** CPCB consent to operate requires stack PM and SO₂ data
- **Epidemiology:** PM₂.₅ and NO₂ linked to cardiovascular mortality (WHO AQG 2021)
- **Process Engineering:** Baghouse / ESP efficiency = $\dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$
- **ESG Reporting:** Real-time CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems) use similar optical/IR detection principles

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Concentration of Particulate Matter from Filter Mass

The fundamental gravimetric principle in Hi-Vol sampling is:

**Given:**
- Initial mass of filter (before sampling): $W_i$ (in grams)
- Final mass of filter (after sampling & conditioning): $W_f$ (in grams)
- Sampling flow rate: $Q$ (in m³/min)
- Sampling duration: $t$ (in minutes)

**Derivation Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Mass of particulate matter collected on the filter

$$
m = W_f - W_i \quad [\text{grams}]
$$

This is purely a balance of mass before and after the sampling run, assuming complete retention by the filter.

**Step 2:** Total volume of air that passed through the filter

$$
V = Q \times t \quad [\text{m}^3]
$$

Here $Q$ is the *time-averaged* flow rate (since filter loading increases back-pressure, the rotameter reading drifts during the run).

**Step 3:** Mass concentration (raw, at sampling conditions)

$$
C_{raw} = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{Q \cdot t} \quad [\text{g/m}^3]
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to micrograms (because air-quality standards use µg/m³)

$$
C = \dfrac{m}{V} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{Q \cdot t} \quad [\mu g/m^3]
$$

The factor $10^6$ comes from: 1 g = $10^6$ µg.

**Step 5 (Optional):** Standardise to NTP (25 °C, 1 atm)

$$
C_{NTP} = C \times \dfrac{P_{sampling}}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{T_{sampling} + 273}
$$

This is essential when comparing data across seasons or altitudes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem:** A Hi-Vol sampler operates at an average flow of **1.2 m³/min** for **8 hours**. The filter paper weighs **3.4521 g** before and **3.5876 g** after. Calculate the TSP concentration. Also convert it to **NTP** if the ambient temperature was **35 °C** and pressure **755 mmHg**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass of dust collected

$$
m = 3.5876 - 3.4521 = 0.1355 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Total volume sampled

$$
V = 1.2 \times (8 \times 60) = 1.2 \times 480 = 576 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Raw concentration

$$
C_{raw} = \dfrac{0.1355}{576} \times 10^6 = 235.24 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Standardise to NTP

$$
C_{NTP} = 235.24 \times \dfrac{755}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{308} = 235.24 \times 0.9934 \times 0.9675
$$

$$
C_{NTP} = 235.24 \times 0.9610 = 226.07 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Inference:** This value exceeds the NAAQS 24-hour TSP limit of **100 µg/m³** (revised standards focus on PM₁₀/PM₂.₅, but legacy TSP was 100–200 µg/m³), indicating a polluted site.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion (West-Gaeke Reference)

The ideal gas equation gives the molar volume at any T, P:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{R T}{P} = 22.414 \text{ L/mol (at 0 °C, 1 atm)}
$$

$$
V_m^{25} = 22.414 \times \dfrac{298}{273} = 24.45 \text{ L/mol (at 25 °C, 1 atm)}
$$

**Conversion from ppm to mg/m³:**

By definition, 1 ppm of gas X means 1 volume of X in $10^6$ volumes of air. Moles per volume:

$$
n = \dfrac{1}{10^6} \text{ mol X per mol air}
$$

Mass of X per m³ of air:

$$
C = \dfrac{MW}{V_m^{25}} \times \dfrac{1}{10^6} \text{ mol} \times \dfrac{10^3 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{MW}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 \text{ per 1 ppm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}\,}
$$

**Example Check:** SO₂ (MW = 64), 1 ppm SO₂ = $64/24.45$ = **2.62 mg/m³**. This is the standard NAAQS unit for SO₂.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sampler Selection

| Criterion | Hi-Vol (HVS) | RDS / Cyclone | Personal Sampler | Detector Tube |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Particle size captured | TSP (≤ 100 µm) | PM₁₀ or PM₂.₅ | PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀ | Gas only |
| Flow rate | High (1.1–1.7 m³/min) | Medium (1 m³/min) | Low (1–4 L/min) | N/A (passive) |
| Sample location | Stationary / shelter | Stationary | Breathing zone | Spot / area |
| Analysis method | Gravimetric | Gravimetric | Gravimetric / chemical | Length-of-stain |
| Sampling time | 8 / 24 hr | 8 / 24 hr | Full shift (8 hr) | Seconds to minutes |
| KTU viva focus | Rotameter reading | d₅₀ cut point | 30 cm rule | Color change end |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 30 cm for personal sampling?** OSHA defines the breathing zone as a hemisphere of radius **30 cm** forward of the shoulders, centred on the nose/mouth. Anything outside this is no longer a personal sample.

---

## 3.5 Calibration Logic of a Rotameter Using a Wet Gas Meter

The rotameter on a Hi-Vol indicates flow at the **sampling line conditions**, not STP. Calibration is performed by attaching a calibrated wet gas meter (or soap-bubble flow meter) in series:

**Procedure Logic:**

1. Set the rotameter float to a specific mark (say 1.2 m³/min equivalent).
2. Simultaneously start the rotameter timer and the wet gas meter.
3. Record the wet gas meter volume for, e.g., 5 minutes.
4. Correct the wet gas meter reading to STP.
5. Compare actual (STP) flow to rotameter indication.
6. Build a **calibration curve** (rotameter reading vs. true flow).

**Calibration factor:**

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{actual, STP}}{V_{rotameter}}
$$

Applied to every sample run:

$$
V_{corrected, STP} = K \times V_{rotameter, indicated}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a High-Volume Air Sampler (Block Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions and signal/flow path** of a Hi-Vol sampler — the most frequently asked instrument in KTU lab exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Air Inlet] --> B[Protective Shelter / Roof]
    B --> C[Filter Paper Holder - Glass Fibre Filter]
    C --> D[Suction Blower Motor 1.1 to 1.7 m3 per min]
    D --> E[Rotameter Flow Indicator]
    E --> F[Pressure Gauge]
    F --> G[Elapsed Time Meter]
    G --> H[Exhaust to Atmosphere]

    P[Pre Sampling Weighing on Analytical Balance 0.1 mg] --> C
    C --> Q[Post Sampling Conditioning 24 h at 25 C 50 percent RH]
    Q --> R[Post Sampling Weighing]
    R --> S[Mass Difference Delta W]
    S --> T[Concentration Calculation C equals Delta W times 10 to the 6 divided by Q times t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The upper chain (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H) represents the **physical airflow path** during sampling. The lower chain (P → C → Q → R → S → T) represents the **analytical flow** that converts gravimetric data into a concentration.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Personal Air Sampling Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Breathing Zone Within 30 cm] --> B[Inlet Cyclone 10 micrometre cut]
    B --> C[Filter Cassette 37 mm]
    C --> D[Critical Orifice or Mass Flow Controller]
    D --> E[Diaphragm Pump Battery Powered]
    E --> F[Exhaust]

    G[Personal Pump Calibration with Bubble Meter] --> D
    H[Worker Worn on Collar] --> A
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Impinger-Based Gas Sampling Train (West-Gaeke SO₂ Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Air] --> B[Probe]
    B --> C[First Impinger 0.04 M TCM Absorber SO2]
    C --> D[Second Impinger Backup Absorber]
    D --> E[Empty Impinger Droplet Trap]
    E --> F[Fritted Glass Filter]
    F --> G[Rotameter]
    G --> H[Suction Pump]
    H --> I[Gas Meter or Volume Totaliser]

    J[Lab Analysis: Pararosaniline Method] --> C
    J --> K[Spectrophotometer Read at 560 nm]
    K --> L[Concentration in microgram per m3]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Hi-Vol vs RDS vs Personal Sampler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hi-Vol Sampler] --> H1[Captures TSP]
    H1 --> H2[Filter 8 x 10 inch]
    H2 --> H3[Flow 1.2 m3 per min]
    H3 --> H4[Outdoor Shelter 5 to 10 m AGL]

    R[RDS / Cyclone Sampler] --> R1[Removes > 10 micrometre]
    R1 --> R2[Filter 8 x 10 inch]
    R2 --> R3[Flow 1.0 m3 per min]
    R3 --> R4[Outdoor or Indoor]

    P[Personal Sampler] --> P1[Cyclone + 37 mm Cassette]
    P1 --> P2[Filter 37 mm]
    P2 --> P3[Flow 1 to 4 L per min]
    P3 --> P4[Breathing Zone]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Literacy:** When asked to *"sketch and label a Hi-Vol sampler"*, always include the **shelter, filter holder, motor, rotameter, and timer**. Skipping the rotameter costs 1 mark; missing the timer costs 0.5 mark.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define air sampling. Differentiate between ambient air sampling and personal air sampling with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Air Sampling:** The process of drawing a known volume of air through a collection medium (filter, sorbent, or absorbing solution) to determine the type and concentration of airborne contaminants.

**Ambient Air Sampling:**

- Purpose: Determine *outdoor* background pollutant levels
- Stationary setup, sampling head placed **5–10 m above ground level**
- Example: Hi-Vol sampler at a CPCB station measuring PM₁₀ and SO₂
- Reference: **NAAQS, 2009 (India) / WHO AQG 2021**

**Personal Air Sampling:**

- Purpose: Assess *worker* exposure in the **breathing zone (within 30 cm)**
- Worn on the collar; battery-operated low-flow pump
- Example: Welding fume sampler on a construction worker (OSHA Method ID-125G)
- Reference: **OSHA PEL, ACGIH TLV**

| Aspect | Ambient | Personal |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Outdoor, fixed | On worker's lapel/collar |
| Distance from source | Variable | 30 cm from nose |
| Flow rate | High (m³/min) | Low (L/min) |
| Compares against | NAAQS | TLV / PEL |

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**With a neat diagram, explain the principle and components of a High Volume Sampler (HVS).**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A blower sucks ambient air at a known high flow rate (1.1–1.7 m³/min) through a glass-fibre filter. Particulates deposit on the filter, which is weighed before and after sampling. The mass difference, divided by the sampled volume, gives the TSP concentration.

**Key Components:**

1. **Aluminium shelter** – houses the unit, prevents rain ingress
2. **Filter holder** – holds 8″ × 10″ glass-fibre filter
3. **Blower motor** – creates suction (centrifugal)
4. **Rotameter** – indicates instantaneous flow
5. **Pressure gauge** – monitors filter loading
6. **Elapsed time meter** – records run duration
7. **Gastight casing** – sealed except for inlet

**Formula Used:**

$$
C_{TSP} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{Q \times t} \text{ in µg/m}^3
$$

> [!TIP]
> Examiners specifically look for: (1) the *suction* principle, (2) the *8×10 filter*, and (3) the *concentration formula*. Omitting any of these loses 1 mark each.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a labelled sketch the working of a **Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)**. How does a cyclone achieve size-selective sampling?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Hi-Vol sampler is run for **10 hours** at an average flow of **1.15 m³/min**. The pre- and post-weights of the filter are **4.2312 g** and **4.3989 g** respectively. If the ambient temperature is **40 °C** and pressure **750 mmHg**, calculate the **NTP-standardised TSP concentration** in **µg/m³**. Comment on its compliance with NAAQS.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**RDS Working (5 marks):**

The RDS is essentially a Hi-Vol sampler fitted with a **cyclone separator** at the inlet. Air enters tangentially into the cyclone, where:

- **Large particles (> 10 µm)** are thrown outward by centrifugal force onto the cyclone wall and collected in a grit pot
- **Fine particles (≤ 10 µm)** escape with the air stream and are captured on the downstream filter paper

**Components of RDS (2 marks):**

- Cyclone head (with 10 µm d₅₀ cut)
- Filter holder (8″ × 10″ glass-fibre)
- Suction pump
- Rotameter + pressure gauge
- Timer
- Aluminium housing

**Cyclone Size Selection Principle:**

The d₅₀ (aerodynamic cut-point) is governed by the **Stairmand equation**:

$$
d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu W}{2 \pi N_c \rho_p (N_s - 1)}}
$$

where $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $W$ = inlet width, $N_c$ = effective number of turns, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $N_s$ = number of turns through vortex finder.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Step 1:** Mass collected

$$
m = 4.3989 - 4.2312 = 0.1677 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Total volume sampled

$$
V = 1.15 \times (10 \times 60) = 1.15 \times 600 = 690 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Raw concentration

$$
C_{raw} = \dfrac{0.1677}{690} \times 10^6 = 243.04 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** NTP standardisation (T = 40 °C = 313 K, P = 750 mmHg)

$$
C_{NTP} = 243.04 \times \dfrac{750}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{313}
$$

$$
C_{NTP} = 243.04 \times 0.9868 \times 0.9521 = 228.36 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Comment

NAAQS legacy TSP 24-hour standard = **100 µg/m³** (industrial) to **200 µg/m³** (residential). Even at the lenient residential limit, **228.36 µg/m³ exceeds the standard**, indicating a polluted site that requires abatement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **West-Gaeke method** for determination of ambient SO₂. List the reagents and write the reaction chemistry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial hygiene survey of a paint factory uses a personal sampler to draw **3.0 L/min** of air through a 37 mm filter for **6 hours**. The filter gains **0.0028 g** of particulate. The worker's **TLV-TWA** for pigment dust is **5 mg/m³**. Comment on compliance.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**West-Gaeke Method (Modified, CPCB Standard):**

Ambient air is drawn at **1 L/min for 24 hours** through an impinger containing **0.04 M Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution, which fixes SO₂ as a stable dichlorosulfitomercurate complex.

**Reaction (Absorption):**

$$
\text{SO}_2 + [\text{HgCl}_4]^{2-} \rightarrow [\text{HgCl}_2 \text{SO}_3]^{2-} + 2 \text{Cl}^-
$$

**Reaction (Analysis – Pararosaniline method):**

$$
[\text{HgCl}_2 \text{SO}_3]^{2-} + \text{Formaldehyde} + \text{Pararosaniline} \rightarrow \text{Red-violet pararosaniline methyl sulfonic acid}
$$

**Absorbance read at 560 nm** on a spectrophotometer, calibrated against standard K₂S₂O₅ solutions.

**Reagents (list):**

1. 0.04 M TCM absorbing solution
2. 0.06 % Pararosaniline dye (PRA)
3. 0.2 % Formaldehyde solution
4. 1 N HCl

**Sensitivity:** ~ **6 µg/m³** of SO₂.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Step 1:** Total volume sampled

$$
V = 3.0 \text{ L/min} \times 6 \times 60 \text{ min} = 1080 \text{ L} = 1.08 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Mass of particulate

$$
m = 0.0028 \text{ g} = 2.8 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 3:** Exposure concentration

$$
C = \dfrac{2.8 \text{ mg}}{1.08 \text{ m}^3} = 2.59 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Compliance

$$
C = 2.59 \text{ mg/m}^3 < TLV_{TWA} = 5 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The worker's exposure is **BELOW the TLV-TWA**. The workplace is in compliance, but the IH professional should note that **short-term excursions** above STEL were not measured by this sample.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit error:** Writing C in g/m³ instead of mg/m³ — full marks loss in part (b)
> 2. **Forgetting 10⁶ factor:** Converting g → µg is worth 1 mark; students often skip this
> 3. **NTP correction:** Some students apply the inversion P/T; examiners deduct 1 mark for direction error
> 4. **Misidentifying cyclone cut-point:** A cyclone with 4 µm d₅₀ collects PM₄, NOT PM₁₀ — read the manufacturer's datasheet
> 5. **Personal sampler 30 cm rule:** Drawing a sampler on a *table* instead of a worker loses 1 mark in viva
> 6. **In West-Gaeke:** Writing "SO₂ dissolved in water" is incorrect — it is fixed as a *mercurate complex*; examiners deduct 1 mark

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module Experiment 2:**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ Air sampling = drawing a *known volume* of air for *qualitative and quantitative* analysis
- ☐ Breathing zone = hemisphere of **30 cm radius** forward of shoulders
- ☐ TSP = Total Suspended Particulates (no size cut)
- ☐ PM₁₀ = Particulate Matter with aerodynamic diameter **≤ 10 µm**
- ☐ PM₂.₅ = **≤ 2.5 µm** (most health-critical)
- ☐ NAAQS = National Ambient Air Quality Standards (India, CPCB 2009)
- ☐ TLV-TWA = Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average (ACGIH, 8-hr)
- ☐ PEL = Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA, 8-hr)

### Key Equipment & Flow Rates
- ☐ Hi-Vol Sampler: **1.1–1.7 m³/min**, 8″ × 10″ filter
- ☐ RDS: **1.0–1.5 m³/min**, cyclone with **10 µm cut**
- ☐ Personal Sampler: **1–4 L/min**, 37 mm cassette, in breathing zone
- ☐ Impinger: ~ **1–2 L/min**, for gases (SO₂, NO₂, NH₃)
- ☐ Detector tube: instantaneous, no pump needed (or hand pump 100 mL)

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ $V = Q \times t$ (Volume from flow and time)
- ☐ $C = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{V}$ (Particulate concentration, µg/m³)
- ☐ $V_s = V \times \dfrac{P}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{T+273}$ (NTP correction)
- ☐ $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ (Gas concentration conversion)
- ☐ Cyclone $d_{50}$ (Stairmand equation)

### Viva-Voce Toppers
- ☐ Why 24-hour sampling duration? → Diurnal variation coverage + NAAQS averaging period
- ☐ Why condition filters at 25 °C / 50 % RH for 24 h? → Equilibrium moisture, reproducible weighing
- ☐ Why glass-fibre filter? → High efficiency (> 99 % at 0.3 µm DOP), low blank mass
- ☐ Difference between active and passive sampling? → Active uses a pump; passive uses molecular diffusion (e.g., Dräger ORSA-5)
- ☐ Why isokinetic sampling in stacks? → To sample particulates at the same velocity as the gas; otherwise, large particles under-sample (oversample) due to inertia
- ☐ What is "Method Blank" in gravimetric analysis? → An unexposed filter handled identically to test filters, used to correct for handling errors and ambient dust

### Standard Reference Books (KTU Approved)
- ☐ W. T. Davis, *Air Pollution Engineering Manual*, 2nd Ed., John Wiley
- ☐ M. N. Rao, H. V. N. Rao, *Air Pollution*, Tata McGraw-Hill
- ☐ S. K. Dhameja, *Environmental Engineering and Management*, S. K. Kataria
- ☐ ACGIH *Industrial Ventilation Manual*, 30th Ed.
- ☐ IS 5182 (Parts I–XXIII) — Indian Standard Methods for Air Quality Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Estimation of Dusts Using High Volume Sampler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL607 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND INDUSTRIAL HYGIENE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Estimation of Dusts Using High Volume Sampler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**High Volume Sampler (HVS)** is a standardized air-quality monitoring instrument used for the gravimetric determination of **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)** and **Respirable Suspended Particulates (RSP / PM₁₀)** present in ambient atmosphere. The device draws a large, known volume of ambient air through a pre-weighed **glass-fibre filter paper** for a defined sampling duration (typically **8 hours or 24 hours**), after which the mass of particulate matter trapped is determined by re-weighing the filter. The mass difference, divided by the volume of air sampled (corrected to standard temperature and pressure), yields the airborne dust concentration expressed in **µg/m³** (micrograms per cubic metre).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The High Volume Sampler is the *reference method* prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India** under the National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP) and is aligned with **IS 5182 (Part XV) – 1974** for atmospheric dust estimation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Vacuum-Cleaner with a Weighing Scale"

Imagine placing a very powerful vacuum-cleaner on a busy roadside. Instead of collecting dust into a transparent bag, the air is sucked through a **special white filter paper** kept inside. After running the machine for, say, 24 hours, the once-white filter turns grey or black because **all the invisible dust floating in the air has been trapped on the paper**.

If we know:
- The filter was **5.000 g** before sampling,
- It became **5.025 g** after sampling,
- The machine drew **1500 litres of air per minute** for 24 hours,

then by simple subtraction and division we can answer: *"How many grams of dust were floating in every cubic metre of air that people on that street were breathing?"*

That single number — **µg of dust per m³ of air** — is the **Ambient Air Quality Index for particulates**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference Values (NAAQMS / CPCB 2009):**
> - **TSP (24-hour average):** ≤ 200 µg/m³ (Industrial), ≤ 100 µg/m³ (Residential/Rural)
> - **PM₁₀ (24-hour average):** ≤ 100 µg/m³ (Industrial), ≤ 60 µg/m³ (Residential)
> - **PM₂.₅ (24-hour average):** ≤ 60 µg/m³ (Industrial), ≤ 40 µg/m³ (Residential)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Nominal Flow Rate (Q) | **1.0 – 1.7 m³/min** (60 – 100 ft³/min) |
| Standard Sampling Duration | **24 ± 1 hours** (8 hrs also accepted) |
| Reference Flow Rate (CPCB) | **1.1 m³/min** at STP |
| Filter Type | **Glass-fibre filter (8″ × 10″)** |
| Particulate Size Captured | **All sizes < 100 µm** (with cyclone: < 10 µm) |
| Reporting Unit | **µg/m³ (micrograms per cubic metre)** |
| Standard Reference | **IS 5182 (Part XV):1974 / CPCB NAAQMS** |

> [!NOTE]
> **STP Conditions:** Standard Temperature = **25 °C (298 K)** and Standard Pressure = **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** are used for volume correction as per Indian Standard practice. (US-EPA uses 25 °C and 101.325 kPa; older AP-42 uses 15 °C.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

The High Volume Sampler works on the principle of **isokinetic suction** combined with **gravimetric retention**. Let us break it down step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Air Aspiration (The "How"):**
A high-capacity blower/motor housed inside the HVS draws ambient air through a protective weather-shield (rain hood) at a calibrated flow rate of approximately **1.1 m³/min**. This large flow rate is essential because airborne particulates are extremely dilute (a few µg per m³), so a large air volume must be sampled to accumulate a measurable mass on the filter.

**Step 2 — Particulate Retention (The "Why"):**
The air stream is forced through a **glass-fibre filter paper** having a typical collection efficiency of **> 99% for particles ≥ 0.3 µm**. Particles larger than the filter pores are retained by:
- **Inertial impaction** (large particles cannot follow the airstream around fibres)
- **Interception** (medium particles graze fibres and stick)
- **Diffusion (Brownian)** (sub-micron particles randomly collide with fibres)
- **Electrostatic attraction** (glass fibres carry a slight electrostatic charge)

**Step 3 — Gravimetric Quantification (The "Result"):**
The mass of captured dust is the difference between post-sampling weight and pre-sampling weight of the filter. The weight is measured on an **analytical balance with 0.1 mg (10⁻⁴ g) sensitivity** under controlled humidity (40–50 % RH) to eliminate moisture interference.

**Step 4 — Concentration Calculation:**
The collected mass is divided by the total volume of air drawn (corrected to STP) to obtain ambient concentration in **µg/m³**.

### 2.2 Why STP Correction is Necessary

The blower flow rate varies with **temperature and barometric pressure** at the sampling site. A flow meter that reads 1.1 m³/min at sea-level on a cool morning will read differently on a hot summer afternoon in Kochi. Hence the **actual sampled volume** must be normalised to STP using the ideal gas law:

$$V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{STP}} \times \frac{T_{STP}}{T_{actual}}$$

where temperatures are in **Kelvin** and pressures in **same units**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m = W_{f} - W_{i}$ | Mass of dust collected on filter | mg or g |
| 2 | $V = Q_{avg} \times t$ | Volume of air sampled (uncorrected) | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{STP} = V \times \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{STP}} \times \dfrac{T_{STP}}{T_{actual}}$ | STP-corrected sampled volume | m³ |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta m \times 10^{6}}{V_{STP}}$ | **Dust concentration in ambient air** | µg/m³ |
| 5 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_{1} + Q_{2} + \cdots + Q_{n}}{n}$ | Average flow rate from rotameter readings | m³/min |
| 6 | $\text{Sampling Efficiency} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{HVS}}{\Delta m_{Reference}} \times 100$ | Compares HVS to reference method | % |
| 7 | $E_{24h} = \dfrac{C_{measured}}{C_{standard}} \times 100$ | % exceedance vs CPCB standard | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember for KTU Exam:**
> - If flow rate is given in **L/min**, convert to **m³/min** by dividing by **1000**.
> - If time is in **hours**, multiply flow (m³/min) by **60** to get m³.
> - If mass is in **mg**, multiply numerator by **10³** to convert µg; if mass is in **g**, multiply by **10⁶**.
> - Pressure in **mm Hg**: 760 mm Hg = 101.325 kPa.
> - Temperature conversion: **T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Regulatory Compliance:** Industries (cement, thermal power, stone-crushers) must self-monitor SPM/PM₁₀ at their fence-line using HVS to comply with Consent-to-Operate conditions of the Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB).
2. **Epidemiological Studies:** Medical researchers correlate HVS-measured PM₂.₅/PM₁₀ with respiratory disease incidence in cities like Delhi, Mumbai, and Kochi.
3. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Baseline air-quality monitoring for new highways, airports, and SEZs.
4. **Industrial Hygiene (Occupational Health):** Modified personal HVS samplers assess worker exposure inside mines and factories.
5. **Source Apportionment:** Particulates collected on filters are chemically analysed (XRF, ICP-MS) to identify pollution sources.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Concentration Equation

We start with the fundamental definition of concentration:

$$C = \frac{\text{Mass of pollutant}}{\text{Volume of air containing it}}$$

**Step 1:** Express mass of dust collected.

The filter paper is weighed before and after sampling. Let:
- $W_i$ = initial weight of filter (g)
- $W_f$ = final weight of filter after sampling (g)

$$\Delta m = W_f - W_i$$

This gives the dust mass in grams.

**Step 2:** Express volume of air sampled.

The HVS blower draws air at a flow rate $Q$ (m³/min) for time $t$ (minutes).

$$V_{actual} = Q_{avg} \times t \quad [\text{in m}^3]$$

**Step 3:** Correct the volume to STP.

Since the HVS flow meter reads the volume at ambient conditions (temperature $T_a$ in Kelvin, pressure $P_a$ in kPa), and we want to compare against the NAAQS which is reported at STP ($T_s = 298$ K, $P_s = 101.325$ kPa), we use the ideal gas relation $PV = nRT$, hence $V \propto T/P$ at constant moles:

$$V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times \frac{P_a}{P_s} \times \frac{T_s}{T_a}$$

**Step 4:** Convert grams to micrograms for reporting.

$$1 \text{ g} = 10^{6} \text{ µg}$$

Therefore mass in µg = $\Delta m \times 10^{6}$.

**Step 5:** Combine everything.

$$\boxed{C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^{6}}{V_{actual} \times \left(\dfrac{P_a}{P_s}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_s}{T_a}\right)}} \quad [\text{µg/m}^3]$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style — 14 Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem Statement:**
A High Volume Sampler is operated at an industrial site in Kochi. The following data were recorded:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Initial weight of filter, $W_i$ | 4.8236 g |
| Final weight of filter, $W_f$ | 4.8481 g |
| Average flow rate, $Q_{avg}$ | 1.12 m³/min |
| Sampling time, $t$ | 8 hours |
| Ambient temperature, $T_a$ | 30 °C |
| Atmospheric pressure, $P_a$ | 101.0 kPa |

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The total volume of air sampled in m³.
**(b)** The STP-corrected volume.
**(c)** The dust concentration in µg/m³.
**(d)** Comment on compliance with CPCB 24-hour NAAQS for TSP.

---

#### **Solution (a): Total Volume of Air Sampled**

Convert time from hours to minutes:
$$t = 8 \text{ hours} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ min}$$

Volume at ambient conditions:
$$V_{actual} = Q_{avg} \times t = 1.12 \times 480$$

$$\boxed{V_{actual} = 537.6 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

#### **Solution (b): STP-Corrected Volume**

Convert temperature to Kelvin:
$$T_a = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$$

Standard temperature:
$$T_s = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}$$

Apply the STP correction formula:
$$V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times \frac{P_a}{P_s} \times \frac{T_s}{T_a}$$

$$V_{STP} = 537.6 \times \frac{101.0}{101.325} \times \frac{298.15}{303.15}$$

Compute each factor separately:
$$\frac{101.0}{101.325} = 0.9968$$
$$\frac{298.15}{303.15} = 0.9835$$

$$V_{STP} = 537.6 \times 0.9968 \times 0.9835$$

$$V_{STP} = 537.6 \times 0.9804$$

$$\boxed{V_{STP} = 527.06 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

#### **Solution (c): Dust Concentration**

Mass of dust collected:
$$\Delta m = W_f - W_i = 4.8481 - 4.8236 = 0.0245 \text{ g}$$

Convert grams to micrograms:
$$\Delta m = 0.0245 \times 10^{6} = 24500 \text{ µg}$$

Apply the master equation:
$$C = \frac{\Delta m}{V_{STP}} = \frac{24500}{527.06}$$

$$\boxed{C = 46.48 \text{ µg/m}^3}$$

---

#### **Solution (d): Compliance Check**

The 24-hour CPCB standard for **TSP in Industrial area = 500 µg/m³**.
(Note: 8-hr sampling is sometimes scaled; the 24-hr standard is the benchmark.)

Measured value = 46.48 µg/m³ (very low; well within NAAQS).

$$\text{Percent of standard} = \frac{46.48}{500} \times 100 = 9.30\%$$

**Conclusion:** The site is **well within compliance**; only ~9.3% of the permissible limit is being used. Good air quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU valuation, marks are deducted if students forget to:
> 1. Convert hours to minutes (Step a)
> 2. Convert °C to K (Step b)
> 3. Use **10⁶ multiplier** for grams→µg (Step c)
> 4. State the CPCB reference value explicitly (Step d)

---

### 3.3 Second Numerical (Quick Practice — Part A Type)

A HVS recorded a net dust mass of 0.0186 g over a sampling duration of 6 hours at an average flow of 1.05 m³/min. The site temperature was 32 °C and pressure was 100.8 kPa. Compute the concentration.

**Solution Sketch:**
- $V_{actual} = 1.05 \times 360 = 378$ m³
- $T_a = 305.15$ K, $T_s = 298.15$ K
- $V_{STP} = 378 \times (100.8/101.325) \times (298.15/305.15) = 378 \times 0.9948 \times 0.9773 = 367.5$ m³
- $\Delta m = 0.0186 \times 10^{6} = 18600$ µg
- $C = 18600 / 367.5 = 50.61$ µg/m³
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a High Volume Sampler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Air with Suspended Dust] --> B[Rain Hood and Weather Shield]
    B --> C[Inlet with 10 cyclone PM10 size selector]
    C --> D[Glass Fibre Filter Paper 8x10 inch on filter holder]
    D --> E[High Capacity Blower Motor 1 to 1.7 m3 per min]
    E --> F[Rotameter Flow Measuring Device]
    F --> G[Pressure Gauge and Temperature Sensor]
    G --> H[Exhaust Air to Atmosphere]
    D --> I[Pre weighed filter W1 in desiccator]
    I --> J[Analytical Balance 0.1 mg sensitivity]
    F --> K[Timer and Totaliser]
    K --> L[Average flow and Total Volume]
    J --> M[Post weighed filter W2]
    L --> N[STP correction using T and P]
    M --> O[Dust Mass delta m = W2 minus W1]
    N --> P[Concentration C in microgram per m3]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Comparison with CPCB NAAQS Standard]
    Q --> R{Compliant?}
    R -- YES --> S[Air Quality Acceptable]
    R -- NO --> T[Implement Control Measures]
```

### 4.2 Component-Wise Functional Description

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Ambient Air Intake | Source of airborne particulate matter |
| B | Rain Hood | Prevents precipitation from wetting the filter |
| C | Inlet / Cyclone (optional) | Selects PM₁₀ size fraction by inertial separation |
| D | Filter Holder + Glass-fibre Filter | Physical collection substrate for dust |
| E | Blower Motor | Provides high suction (typically 0.5–1.5 HP) |
| F | Rotameter | Indicates instantaneous flow rate |
| G | T & P Sensors | Allow STP correction of sampled volume |
| H | Exhaust | Cleaned air released back to environment |
| I, J, M | Weighing System | Quantifies dust mass gravimetrically |
| K, L | Timer / Totaliser | Integrates flow over time → total volume |
| N, O, P | Calculation Block | Applies master formula to obtain C |
| Q, R, S, T | Compliance Decision | Compares result with NAAQS for action |

### 4.3 Typical 24-Hour Sampling Time-Profile

```mermaid
gantt
    title HVS 24 Hour Sampling Operation Timeline
    dateFormat HH:mm
    axisFormat %H:%M
    section Pre Sampling
    Filter Conditioning in Desiccator 24 hr :done, des1, 00:00, 2h
    Initial Weighing W1 :done, w1, 02:00, 1h
    section Sampling
    Filter Loading and Sealing :active, load, 03:00, 30m
    HVS Operation 24 hour run :crit, run, 03:30, 24h
    section Post Sampling
    Filter Retrieval and Conditioning :cond, 04:30, 2h
    Final Weighing W2 :w2, 06:30, 1h
    section Analysis
    Calculation of C :calc, 07:30, 2h
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
*List any three components of a High Volume Sampler and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Glass-fibre filter paper (8″ × 10″):** Physically retains suspended particulate matter from the air stream by impaction, interception, and diffusion mechanisms. (1 mark)
2. **High-capacity blower motor:** Draws ambient air through the filter at a calibrated flow rate of ~1.1 m³/min. (1 mark)
3. **Rotameter (flow meter):** Provides instantaneous indication of volumetric flow rate so the operator can adjust the suction to maintain isokinetic sampling. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Why is STP correction applied to the sampled air volume in HVS analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The HVS rotameter reads the air volume at *ambient* temperature and pressure, which vary between sites and seasons. Since the CPCB NAAQS standards are reported at STP (25 °C, 101.325 kPa), the sampled volume must be normalised using the ideal-gas relation (1 mark). The correction formula is  
$V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times (P_a/P_s) \times (T_s/T_a)$ (1 mark).  
This ensures **comparability** of concentration values across different geographic locations and climatic conditions (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students frequently forget the **×10⁶ conversion** (g → µg). This single error costs 2 marks.
> - Temperature **must** be in **Kelvin** in the STP formula. °C values give wrong answers and 0 marks for that sub-part.
> - Always **state the CPCB standard value** you are comparing against; otherwise the "comment" sub-part gets zero.
> - Show **units at every step**. A final answer without the unit (µg/m³) loses 0.5 mark.
> - Do **not** confuse TSP (Total Suspended Particulates) with PM₁₀; they are different size fractions and different standards.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle, components, and working of a High Volume Sampler** for the determination of Total Suspended Particulates in ambient air. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HVS was operated at a stone-crusher site with the following data:  
$W_i = 3.9742$ g, $W_f = 4.0398$ g, $Q_{avg} = 1.08$ m³/min, $t = 24$ hours, $T_a = 31$ °C, $P_a = 100.9$ kPa.  
Calculate (i) the total air volume sampled, (ii) the STP-corrected volume, (iii) the TSP concentration in µg/m³, and (iv) comment on compliance with CPCB NAAQS for industrial area. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The High Volume Sampler operates on the principle of **gravimetric collection** of airborne particulates on a glass-fibre filter. Ambient air enters through a **rain hood** (1) which prevents moisture damage. It then passes through a **filter holder** (2) containing a pre-weighed glass-fibre filter where particulates get retained. A **suction blower** (3) of about 0.5–1.5 HP capacity draws air at a controlled flow of 1.1 m³/min. The flow is monitored continuously by a **rotameter** (4) and the cumulative volume is integrated by a **gas totaliser/timer** (5). Temperature and pressure sensors (6) allow STP correction of the sampled volume.

After 24 hours, the filter is carefully removed, conditioned in a desiccator for 24 h to remove moisture (7), and reweighed. The difference $W_f - W_i$ gives the dust mass. The TSP concentration is then:
$$C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^{6}}{V_{actual} \times (P_a/P_s) \times (T_s/T_a)} \text{ µg/m}^3$$

This value is compared with CPCB NAAQS (TSP = 500 µg/m³ for 24-h industrial). (Award marks for diagram 3 + description 4 = 7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Total Volume Sampled:**
$$t = 24 \times 60 = 1440 \text{ min}$$
$$V_{actual} = 1.08 \times 1440 = 1555.2 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(ii) STP-Corrected Volume:**
$$T_a = 31 + 273.15 = 304.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_s = 298.15 \text{ K}$$
$$V_{STP} = 1555.2 \times \frac{100.9}{101.325} \times \frac{298.15}{304.15} \quad \text{[setup 1 mark]}$$
$$V_{STP} = 1555.2 \times 0.9958 \times 0.9803 = 1518.6 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{[calculation 1 mark]}$$

**(iii) TSP Concentration:**
$$\Delta m = 4.0398 - 3.9742 = 0.0656 \text{ g} = 65600 \text{ µg}$$
$$C = \frac{65600}{1518.6} = 43.20 \text{ µg/m}^3 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Compliance Comment:**
CPCB industrial-area 24-h TSP standard = **500 µg/m³** (1 mark for stating the value).
Measured value 43.20 µg/m³ is **8.6%** of the standard — well within compliance. (1 mark for comparison)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **significance of STP correction** in HVS-based air-quality monitoring. Derive the STP correction formula starting from the ideal-gas equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential colony in Thiruvananthapuram reported the following HVS data:  
$W_i = 4.5120$ g, $W_f = 4.5315$ g, average rotameter readings at start and end were 1.20 and 1.05 m³/min respectively, sampling time = 8 hours, $T_a = 29$ °C, $P_a = 101.2$ kPa.  
Compute (i) average flow rate, (ii) STP-corrected volume, (iii) dust concentration, and (iv) check compliance against CPCB residential-area TSP standard. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

The volume recorded by the HVS rotameter is at **ambient** temperature $T_a$ and pressure $P_a$, but the NAAQS standards prescribed by CPCB are reported at **STP** ($T_s = 298.15$ K, $P_s = 101.325$ kPa) (1 mark).

**Derivation from Ideal Gas Law** (4 marks):
$$PV = nRT \implies V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$

For a fixed mass of air (n = constant) at two states (actual → STP):
$$\frac{V_{actual}}{T_a / P_a} = \frac{V_{STP}}{T_s / P_s}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times \frac{T_s}{T_a} \times \frac{P_a}{P_s}$$

Substituting into the master concentration equation:
$$C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^{6}}{V_{actual} \times (T_s/T_a) \times (P_a/P_s)} \text{ µg/m}^3$$

This correction is essential for **uniform comparison** of pollution data across geographically diverse CPCB stations (coastal, hill, desert) (2 marks).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**(i) Average Flow Rate:**
$$Q_{avg} = \frac{1.20 + 1.05}{2} = 1.125 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(ii) STP-Corrected Volume:**
$$V_{actual} = 1.125 \times (8 \times 60) = 1.125 \times 480 = 540 \text{ m}^3$$
$$T_a = 29 + 273.15 = 302.15 \text{ K}$$
$$V_{STP} = 540 \times \frac{101.2}{101.325} \times \frac{298.15}{302.15} = 540 \times 0.9988 \times 0.9868 = 532.1 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Dust Concentration:**
$$\Delta m = 4.5315 - 4.5120 = 0.0195 \text{ g} = 19500 \text{ µg}$$
$$C = \frac{19500}{532.1} = 36.65 \text{ µg/m}^3 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Compliance:**
CPCB residential 24-h TSP standard = **200 µg/m³** (1 mark for value).
$C = 36.65$ µg/m³ is **18.3%** of standard — fully compliant. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember (Must-Know for KTU Viva & Exam):**

- **HVS** = High Volume Sampler; reference instrument for **TSP** and **PM₁₀** measurement.
- Standard flow rate ≈ **1.1 m³/min** (60–100 ft³/min); sampling duration **8 h or 24 h**.
- Filter is **glass-fibre, 8″ × 10″**; weighed on **0.1 mg analytical balance** after 24-h desiccation.
- Master formula: $C = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^{6}}{V_{actual} \times (P_a/P_s) \times (T_s/T_a)}$ — units **µg/m³**.
- **STP conditions**: T = 25 °C (298.15 K), P = 101.325 kPa.
- Always convert **°C → K** (add 273.15), **g → µg** (multiply by 10⁶), **hours → minutes** (multiply by 60).
- **CPCB 24-h NAAQS for TSP**: Industrial = 500 µg/m³; Residential/Rural = 200 µg/m³; Sensitive = 100 µg/m³.
- **CPCB 24-h NAAQS for PM₁₀**: Industrial = 100 µg/m³; Residential = 60 µg/m³; Sensitive = 60 µg/m³.
- Indian Standard reference: **IS 5182 (Part XV):1974** — Methods for Measurement of Air Pollution (Suspended Particulates).
- Particulate collection mechanisms: **Inertial impaction, Interception, Diffusion, Electrostatic attraction**.
- HVS limitations: No size segregation (gives TSP only); for PM₁₀/PM₂.₅ a **cyclone inlet** or **impactor** must be attached.
- The flow rate **decreases** during sampling because the filter loads up with dust → use **average of start and end readings**.
- Sources of error: Moisture on filter (controlled by desiccator), wind speed > 20 km/h, filter handling loss, balance drift.
- Always wear **gloves and forceps** while handling the filter — skin oils can alter the weight.
- **Siting criteria** (CPCB): height 3–10 m above ground, away from obstructions by ≥ 2× height of obstacle, away from direct emission sources by ≥ 50 m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Estimation of Dust Using Personal Sampler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL607 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND INDUSTRIAL HYGIENE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Estimation of Dust Using Personal Sampler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Personal Dust Sampling** is a standard industrial hygiene technique used to assess the exposure of an individual worker to airborne particulate matter (dust) in the occupational workplace. It involves mounting a portable sampling device in the worker's **breathing zone** (the region within a hemisphere of 30 cm radius extending in front of the face, centered on the midpoint of an imaginary line joining the ears) and drawing a measured volume of air through a pre-weighed filter media using a battery-operated personal sampling pump. The mass of dust collected is determined gravimetrically, and the airborne dust concentration is expressed in **mg/m³**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment aligns with OSHA Method *NIOSH 0500* (Total Dust) and *NIOSH 0600* (Respirable Dust). The cyclone separator follows the **BMRC (British Medical Research Council)** or **ACGIH** criterion with a 4 µm cut-point for respirable fraction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tiny **vacuum cleaner worn on a worker's collar** — but instead of sucking up visible crumbs, it silently and continuously traps microscopic dust particles onto a paper disc inside it. By the end of the shift, the worker has been breathing potentially hazardous dust, while the disc has been **"catching a sample" of the exact same air** that the worker inhaled. We then weigh that disc before and after — just like weighing a sponge before and after wiping a dusty table — and from the difference in weight and the volume of air pulled through, we know exactly **how much dust was in the breathing air**. If that amount exceeds safety limits, the workplace needs better ventilation or the worker needs respiratory protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standards used in this experiment:**
> - **Density of air at STP** = 1.2 kg/m³
> - **Standard flow rate for total dust** = 1.0 to 4.0 L/min (typically **2.0 L/min**)
> - **Standard flow rate for respirable dust** = **1.7 L/min**
> - **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** for inert dust = **10 mg/m³** (8-hr TWA)
> - **TLV-TWA (Respirable fraction)** = **3 mg/m³**
> - **Sampling time** = Full-shift (≥ 7 hours) or minimum 80% of shift duration

### 1.3 Scope of the Experiment

The objectives as per KTU 2024 lab manual include:
1. To determine the **total dust concentration** and/or **respirable dust concentration** in the workplace air.
2. To compare measured concentration with **statutory Threshold Limit Values (TLVs)**.
3. To evaluate the adequacy of existing control measures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation — The "Why" and "How"

A Personal Sampler mimics the human respiratory uptake of dust by segregating particles based on their **aerodynamic diameter** ($d_a$), which governs how deeply they penetrate the lungs:

- **Inhalable fraction:** Particles with $d_a \leq 100\ \mu m$ enter the nose and mouth.
- **Thoracic fraction:** $d_a \leq 25\ \mu m$ penetrate past the larynx.
- **Respirable fraction:** $d_a \leq 10\ \mu m$ reach the alveolar region (gas-exchange zone).

The personal sampler exploits an **inertial separation principle** via a **10 mm nylon cyclone** to mimic the lung's filtering action. Larger particles (too heavy to follow the curved air stream) are thrown against the cyclone wall and collected in a **"grit pot"**, while smaller (respirable) particles follow the vortex and are trapped on a **filter cassette** (typically a 37 mm PVC or MCE filter of 5.0 µm pore size).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Calibration of Pump:** The flow rate of the personal sampling pump is set and verified **before and after** sampling using a **calibrated rotameter/bubble meter** to ensure accuracy. This compensates for battery drain and filter loading.

**Step 2 — Pre-weighing of Filter:** The filter is conditioned in a **desiccator** for 24 hours to remove moisture, then weighed on an **analytical balance** (sensitivity = 0.01 mg or 0.001 mg) to obtain the **initial mass** ($W_1$).

**Step 3 — Field Sampling:** The sampler is clipped to the worker's collar in the breathing zone. Air is drawn at constant flow rate $Q$ for a known time $t$.

**Step 4 — Post-weighing of Filter:** After sampling, the filter is re-conditioned (24 hr desiccation) and reweighed to obtain **final mass** ($W_2$).

**Step 5 — Computation:** The mass of dust retained = $W_2 - W_1$. Dividing this by the air volume sampled $V = Q \times t$ yields the **time-weighted average (TWA) concentration**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Mining & Quarrying | Monitoring silica dust in drillers, crusher operators |
| Cement Industry | Evaluating baghouse efficiency and worker exposure |
| Welding Shops | Quantifying metal fume exposure |
| Pharmaceutical Plants | Assessing active ingredient aerosol exposure |
| Construction Sites | Compliance with OSHA Respirable Crystalline Silica Standard (29 CFR 1926.1153) |
| Textile Mills | Cotton dust exposure (Byssinosis prevention) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exam — Master These**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dust Concentration}\ C &= \frac{W_2 - W_1}{V} = \frac{\Delta W}{Q \times t} \quad [\text{mg/m}^3] \\[4pt]
\text{Sampled Volume}\ V &= Q \times t \quad [\text{L or m}^3] \\[4pt]
\text{Time-Weighted Average (TWA)} &= \frac{C_1 t_1 + C_2 t_2 + \dots + C_n t_n}{t_1 + t_2 + \dots + t_n} \\[4pt]
\text{Exceedance Fraction (EF)} &= \frac{C_{\text{measured}} - \text{TLV}}{\text{TLV}} \times 100\ \% \\[4pt]
\text{Compliance Ratio (CR)} &= \frac{C_{\text{measured}}}{\text{PEL/TLV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Boundary / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | Initial filter mass | mg | Pre-weighed after 24-hr desiccation |
| $W_2$ | Final filter mass | mg | Post-weighed after 24-hr desiccation |
| $\Delta W$ | Mass of dust collected | mg | $\Delta W = W_2 - W_1$ |
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | L/min | $1.7$ (respirable) $\vert$ $2.0$ (total) |
| $t$ | Sampling duration | min | Full shift, $\geq 420$ min |
| $V$ | Sampled air volume | L or m³ | $V = Q \cdot t$ |
| $C$ | Dust concentration | mg/m³ | Compare with TLV |
| TLV | Threshold Limit Value | mg/m³ | $3$ (resp.) $\vert$ $10$ (total) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Dust Concentration Formula

The fundamental relationship is rooted in the **definition of mass concentration**:

> *Concentration is the mass of contaminant per unit volume of carrier fluid (air).*

**Logical Step 1:** The mass of dust captured by the filter is the difference between the post-sampling mass and the pre-sampling mass of the filter. Any increase in mass is entirely attributable to deposited dust (assuming complete retention of particles by the filter media).

$$
\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 \quad [\text{mg}]
$$

**Logical Step 2:** The volume of air that passed through the filter is the product of the volumetric flow rate $Q$ and the elapsed time $t$. This follows from the kinematic identity $V = Q \cdot t$ (analogous to distance = speed × time).

$$
V = Q \times t
$$

**Logical Step 3:** If $Q$ is measured in **L/min** and $t$ in **minutes**, the resulting $V$ will be in **liters**. To convert to cubic meters, we apply the unit conversion $1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L}$.

$$
V \ [\text{m}^3] = \frac{Q \times t}{1000}
$$

**Logical Step 4:** By the definition of concentration (mass ÷ volume), the airborne dust concentration $C$ is the ratio of the captured dust mass to the sampled air volume.

$$
C = \frac{\Delta W}{V}
$$

**Logical Step 5:** Substituting $V$ from Step 3 into Step 4 gives the working equation in SI-consistent units:

$$
C \ \left[\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{m}^3}\right] = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{Q \times t} \times 1000
$$

where $Q$ is in L/min, $t$ is in min, and $\Delta W$ is in mg.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Model Board-Standard Solution)

**Problem Statement:** A worker in a cement bagging unit wears a personal dust sampler for a full 8-hour shift. The pump operates at $Q = 2.0$ L/min. The initial filter mass is $W_1 = 38.452$ mg and the final filter mass after sampling is $W_2 = 40.127$ mg. Determine: (a) the sampled air volume, (b) the total dust concentration in mg/m³, and (c) whether the exposure complies with the OSHA PEL of 10 mg/m³ for total dust.

**Given Data:**

$$
Q = 2.0\ \text{L/min}, \quad t = 8\ \text{hr}, \quad W_1 = 38.452\ \text{mg}, \quad W_2 = 40.127\ \text{mg}
$$

**Step (a): Sampled Air Volume**

Convert time to minutes:

$$
t = 8\ \text{hr} \times 60\ \frac{\text{min}}{\text{hr}} = 480\ \text{min}
$$

Apply the volume formula:

$$
V = Q \times t = 2.0\ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}} \times 480\ \text{min} = 960\ \text{L}
$$

Convert to cubic meters:

$$
V = \frac{960}{1000} = 0.960\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step (b): Total Dust Concentration**

Compute mass of dust captured:

$$
\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 40.127 - 38.452 = 1.675\ \text{mg}
$$

Apply the concentration formula:

$$
C = \frac{\Delta W}{V} = \frac{1.675\ \text{mg}}{0.960\ \text{m}^3} = 1.745\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

**Step (c): Compliance Check**

Compute the **Compliance Ratio**:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{C_{\text{measured}}}{\text{PEL}} = \frac{1.745}{10.0} = 0.1745
$$

Since $\text{CR} < 1.0$, the exposure is **within permissible limits**. The workplace is **COMPLIANT** with OSHA standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A CR of 0.17 means the worker is exposed to only ~17.4 % of the legally allowable dust. This suggests the existing engineering controls (ventilation, dust suppression) are adequate — though periodic monitoring must continue to detect any process drift.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Dust Type

| Observation During Sampling | Likely Cause | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| $W_2 < W_1$ (negative mass) | Filter lost moisture / handling loss; balance drift | Recondition, reweigh; invalidate sample |
| $C \gg \text{TLV}$ (> 3×) | Source of generation uncontrolled | Engineering controls: local exhaust ventilation, enclosure, wet suppression |
| $C \approx \text{TLV}$ | Marginal compliance | Administrative controls: job rotation, PPE upgrade |
| Filter appears discoloured | Possible presence of metal fumes, silica, soot | Trigger bulk sample analysis (XRD for silica) |
| Pump flow rate drops > 5% | Filter loading / battery drain | Re-calibrate; flag sample as suspect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Personal Sampling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air in Breathing Zone] --> B[Inlet Cassette with 10 mm Cyclone Separator]
    B --> C[Grit Pot: Collects Non-Respirable Dust d_a greater than 10 micrometer]
    B --> D[Filter Holder with 37 mm PVC Filter 5 micrometer pore]
    D --> E[Air Exit to Pump Inlet]
    E --> F[Battery Operated Personal Sampling Pump with Flow Control]
    F --> G[Rotameter or Bubble Flow Meter for Calibration]
    G --> H[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    D --> I[Post Sampling Gravimetric Analysis on Analytical Balance]
    I --> J[Compute Concentration in mg per cubic meter]
    J --> K[Compare with TLV or PEL]
    K --> L{Compliance Decision}
    L -- Yes --> M[Within Statutory Limits]
    L -- No --> N[Implement Engineering and Administrative Controls]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Process Flowchart of the Complete Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Desiccate Filter 24 hr] --> P2[Step 2: Weigh Filter on Analytical Balance Obtain W1]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Calibrate Personal Pump using Rotameter]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Load Filter into Cassette Attach to Cyclone]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Clip Sampler to Worker Collar in Breathing Zone]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Run Pump for Full Shift Duration Record t]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Remove Filter Carefully Avoid Contamination]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Desiccate Loaded Filter 24 hr]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Reweigh Obtain W2]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Calculate C equals W2 minus W1 divided by Q times t times 1000]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Compare C with TLV PEL]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Report Result and Recommend Controls if Exceeded]
```

### 4.3 Component Schematic — Cyclone–Filter–Pump Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Air Inlet at Breathing Zone] --> A2[Cyclone Body with Tangential Entry]
    A2 --> A3[Outer Vortex with Larger Particles Thrown Outward]
    A3 --> A4[Grit Pot at Cyclone Bottom Collects Coarse Dust]
    A2 --> A5[Inner Vortex with Respirable Dust less than 10 micrometer]
    A5 --> A6[Filter Cassette with 37 mm Membrane Filter]
    A6 --> A7[Pump Suction Line]
    A7 --> A8[Diaphragm Pump with DC Motor]
    A8 --> A9[Flow Regulating Orifice]
    A9 --> A10[Exhaust Orifice to Atmosphere]
    A6 -.-> A11[Gravimetric Analysis: W1 to W2]
```

### 4.4 Graphical Representation of Concentration vs. Threshold

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Measured Concentration C_m] --> Comp{Is C_m less than TLV}
    Comp -- Yes --> Safe[Workplace is COMPLIANT]
    Comp -- No --> Unsafe[Workplace is NON-COMPLIANT]
    Unsafe --> Action[Implement Local Exhaust Ventilation, PPE, Wet Dust Suppression]
    Action --> ReMon[Re-monitor After 30 Days]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

Define the term **"breathing zone"** as applicable to personal dust sampling. Why is the sampler placed in this region and not at a fixed corner of the room?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The breathing zone is defined by **ACGIH** as the hemisphere of **30 cm radius** extending in front of the face, centered on the midpoint of an imaginary line joining the two ears of the worker.
- **Significance (1 Mark):** It represents the actual air mass inhaled by the worker during occupational tasks.
- **Reason for placement (0.5 Mark):** Room-corner sampling represents **area or ambient** monitoring, which underestimates or overestimates personal exposure depending on the worker's mobility. Breathing zone sampling yields the **true personal exposure** and is the only legally accepted method under OSHA/ACGIH norms for compliance with PELs.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

Differentiate between **Total Dust** and **Respirable Dust** sampling. Mention the cyclone used for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Total Dust | Respirable Dust |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | All airborne particles collected on filter | Particles with $d_a \leq 10\ \mu m$ reaching alveoli |
| Sampler | Closed-face 37 mm cassette | **10 mm nylon cyclone** |
| Flow rate | $1.0$ to $4.0$ L/min (typ. $2.0$) | **$1.7$ L/min** |
| Cut-point | None (all sizes) | $4.0\ \mu m$ (50% efficiency) |
| TLV-TWA (ACGIH) | $10$ mg/m³ (inert dust) | $3$ mg/m³ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the **components and working of a Personal Dust Sampler**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a foundry workshop, a personal dust sampler was operated for 6.5 hours at a flow rate of 1.7 L/min. The initial and final weights of the filter were 42.318 mg and 44.092 mg respectively. Calculate the **respirable dust concentration** and comment on the compliance with the ACGIH TLV of 3 mg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Components and Working (7 Marks)

A personal dust sampler consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Inlet Head / Cyclone Separator (2 Marks):**
   - A **10 mm nylon cyclone** acts as a size-selective pre-separator.
   - Air enters tangentially, creating a vortex. Due to inertia, particles with $d_a > 10\ \mu m$ are flung outward to the cyclone wall and collected in a removable **"grit pot"** at the bottom.
   - Smaller respirable particles ($d_a \leq 10\ \mu m$) follow the inner vortex upward and exit through the central outlet to the filter.

2. **Filter Cassette Assembly (1.5 Marks):**
   - A **37 mm diameter PVC filter** (pore size $5.0\ \mu m$) housed in a three-piece plastic cassette.
   - Captures respirable particulate matter for gravimetric analysis.

3. **Personal Sampling Pump (2 Marks):**
   - Battery-operated, constant-flow diaphragm pump (typical flow range $1$–$5$ L/min).
   - Equipped with a **flow control valve** and **rotameter** for setting/verifying flow.

4. **Tubing and Clip (1 Mark):**
   - Flexible Tygon tubing connects cyclone → cassette → pump.
   - The entire assembly is clipped to the worker's **lapel/collar** within the breathing zone.

5. **Calibration Assembly (0.5 Mark):**
   - A **calibrated bubble meter or electronic flow calibrator** is attached to the inlet pre- and post-sampling to verify the flow rate remains within $\pm 5\%$ of set value.

**Working Principle (Block diagram drawn by student):** Atmospheric air → Cyclone (removes non-respirable fraction) → Filter (traps respirable dust) → Pump (draws air) → Flow meter (calibration) → Exhaust.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**

$$
Q = 1.7\ \text{L/min},\ t = 6.5\ \text{hr},\ W_1 = 42.318\ \text{mg},\ W_2 = 44.092\ \text{mg},\ \text{TLV} = 3\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 1:** Convert sampling time to minutes:

$$
t = 6.5 \times 60 = 390\ \text{min}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute sampled volume:

$$
V = Q \times t = 1.7 \times 390 = 663\ \text{L} = 0.663\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute mass of dust collected:

$$
\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 44.092 - 42.318 = 1.774\ \text{mg}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute concentration:

$$
C = \frac{\Delta W}{V} = \frac{1.774}{0.663} = 2.676\ \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 2.68\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Compliance check:

$$
\text{Compliance Ratio} = \frac{2.68}{3.0} = 0.893
$$

Since $\text{CR} = 0.893 < 1.0$, the exposure is **within TLV**. **However**, it is dangerously close (89.3% of limit) and warrants **periodic re-monitoring** and **administrative controls** to prevent drift into non-compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *— Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of inertial separation** in a cyclone. Derive the relationship between the **50% cut-point aerodynamic diameter** ($d_{50}$) and cyclone dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a shift, a worker's personal sampler for total dust was operated at $Q = 2.5$ L/min for 7.5 hours. The filter mass increased from 51.246 mg to 55.879 mg. Compute the **TWA concentration** and state whether respiratory protection is mandatory, given the ACGIH TLV of 10 mg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Inertial Separation Principle (7 Marks)

**Principle (3 Marks):**
Inertial separation exploits the fact that **larger particles have higher inertia** and cannot follow the curved streamlines of a swirling air stream. In a cyclone, air enters tangentially and spirals downward along the inner wall. Particles with high mass (large $d_a$) are flung outward by centrifugal force and impact the wall, losing momentum and sliding down into the grit pot. Smaller particles, having low inertia, remain entrained in the air stream and are carried inward to the filter.

**Derivation of $d_{50}$ (4 Marks):**

The 50% cut-point is defined as the aerodynamic diameter at which the cyclone has 50% collection efficiency. It is governed by the **Stokes number** ($Stk$), equating the centrifugal settling velocity to the radial gas velocity.

For a particle in a rotating flow, the centrifugal acceleration is $v_t^2 / r$, where $v_t$ is the tangential velocity and $r$ is the cyclone radius. Setting the radial drift velocity equal to the inlet velocity gives:

$$
d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9\ \eta\ b\ B}{2\ \pi\ N_e\ \rho_p\ v_t\ (D - b)}}
$$

where $\eta$ = air viscosity, $b$ = inlet width, $B$ = body diameter, $N_e$ = effective number of turns, $\rho_p$ = particle density (assumed $1\ \text{g/cm}^3$ for unit-density spheres), $D$ = cyclone diameter, $v_t$ = inlet velocity.

For the standard **10 mm nylon cyclone** at 1.7 L/min, the empirical value of $d_{50} = 4.0\ \mu m$, which corresponds to the **BMRC criterion** for the respirable fraction.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**

$$
Q = 2.5\ \text{L/min},\ t = 7.5\ \text{hr},\ W_1 = 51.246\ \text{mg},\ W_2 = 55.879\ \text{mg},\ \text{TLV} = 10\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 1:** Sampling duration in minutes:

$$
t = 7.5 \times 60 = 450\ \text{min}
$$

**Step 2:** Sampled volume:

$$
V = 2.5 \times 450 = 1125\ \text{L} = 1.125\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of dust:

$$
\Delta W = 55.879 - 51.246 = 4.633\ \text{mg}
$$

**Step 4:** Concentration:

$$
C = \frac{4.633}{1.125} = 4.118\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Compliance check:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{4.118}{10.0} = 0.412
$$

Since $\text{CR} < 1.0$, the exposure is **COMPLIANT**. Respiratory protection is **not legally mandatory**, but as a **best practice** in industrial hygiene, the use of **N95 filtering facepiece respirators** is recommended because total dust at 41% of TLV is still a significant exposure that may contain trace silica or heavy metals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Students frequently forget to convert $V$ from liters to m³. The factor of **1000** in the denominator of the concentration formula is the most common source of arithmetic error. Always write: $C = \frac{\Delta W \times 1000}{Q \cdot t}$ with $Q$ in L/min and $t$ in min, yielding $C$ in mg/m³.
> 2. **Negative $\Delta W$ confusion:** If $W_2 < W_1$, students compute a negative concentration. The correct response is to **invalidate the sample** and re-run; do not report a negative value.
> 3. **Forgetting desiccation:** Markers deduct up to 1 mark if students omit the 24-hr desiccation step before each weighing.
> 4. **Cyclone confusion:** Students often confuse respirable (1.7 L/min with cyclone) with total (2.0 L/min without cyclone). Specifying the wrong flow rate for the wrong dust type is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **CR vs. EF confusion:** Compliance Ratio (CR) is dimensionless; Exceedance Fraction (EF) is expressed as a percentage. KTU expects CR for the standard "compliance" question.
> 6. **Balance sensitivity:** Always state the balance sensitivity (0.01 mg or 0.001 mg) and the desiccator conditions — these are the "labmanship" marks (typically 1 mark) that differentiate a 12/14 from a 14/14 answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Breathing zone** = 30 cm hemisphere in front of face (ACGIH definition).
- ☐ **Total dust** → 37 mm cassette only → typical flow $2.0$ L/min.
- ☐ **Respirable dust** → 10 mm nylon cyclone + 37 mm filter → flow $1.7$ L/min.
- ☐ **ACGIH TLV-TWA (Total inert dust)** = 10 mg/m³; **(Respirable)** = 3 mg/m³.
- ☐ **PEL** = Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA); **TLV** = Threshold Limit Value (ACGIH).
- ☐ **Cut-point** of 10 mm nylon cyclone = 4.0 µm at 1.7 L/min (BMRC criterion).
- ☐ **Formula:** $C = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{Q \times t}$ with $C$ in mg/m³, $Q$ in L/min, $t$ in min.
- ☐ **Desiccation** for 24 hours **before** both pre- and post-weighing is mandatory.
- ☐ **Balance** sensitivity must be at least **0.01 mg** (preferably 0.001 mg).
- ☐ **Calibration** of pump done **before and after** sampling using a **bubble meter or primary standard**.
- ☐ Flow rate must remain within $\pm 5\%$ of the set value throughout sampling.
- ☐ Sampling duration should be **≥ 80%** of full shift (≥ 7 hours for 8-hr shift) for TWA validity.
- ☐ If $\text{CR} > 1$ → **non-compliant** → engineering controls + PPE required.
- ☐ **Negative $\Delta W$** = invalid sample → repeat, never report negative concentration.
- ☐ **Hygroscopic filters** (e.g., MCE) need rigorous desiccation; PVC filters are less sensitive.
- ☐ Always report results with **sampling time, flow rate, and analytical method** for traceability.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Personal Dust Sampling**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based framework. All formulas, constants, and procedures conform to NIOSH 0500/0600 and ACGIH industrial hygiene standards.*

topics.title: Measurement of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFL607 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND INDUSTRIAL HYGIENE LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Noise # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Noise** is defined as any unwanted, unpleasant, or harmful sound that disrupts normal activities, communication, or rest, and may adversely affect human health and well-being. In the context of **Industrial Hygiene** and **Environmental Engineering**, noise is measured as the fluctuating acoustic pressure superimposed on the ambient atmospheric pressure, quantified using standardized logarithmic scales. The **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, denoted $L_p$, is the fundamental measurable quantity, expressed in **decibels (dB)**, referenced to the threshold of human hearing $p_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ where: - $p_{rms}$ = Root Mean Square (RMS) value of the acoustic pressure (Pa) - $p_0$ = Reference pressure = **$20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$** (20 µPa) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The standard reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$ corresponds to the average threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz, as defined by **ISO 389-7** and adopted by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 7194)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a quiet library. A whisper is barely audible — that is a low sound pressure. Now imagine a jet engine starting 10 meters away — the air physically vibrates your eardrums. The difference between these two is not simply "ten times louder"; human hearing is **logarithmic**, not linear. To capture this, engineers use the **decibel (dB)** scale. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Volume Ruler":** > Think of a ruler. Each marking on a ruler adds 1 cm in *equal* (linear) steps. Now imagine a ruler where each mark represents a *multiplication* of the previous value (×1.12 each step). That is a logarithmic scale.
