> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **acute** and **chronic** industrial fatigue.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Acute Fatigue | Chronic Fatigue |
|---|---|---|
| Onset | Sudden, within a shift | Gradual, over weeks/months |
| Relief | Relieved by single adequate rest | Persists despite rest |
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Partially reversible, may need medical care |
| Cause | Single heavy work bout | Sustained overexertion, poor rest cycles |
| Example | End-of-shift tiredness in foundry worker | Long-term exhaustion in night-shift nurses |

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — ESE Module 2]**

### OR

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A(a).** Describe in detail the **physiological methods** of studying industrial fatigue. Include the **Kraepelin work curve** and the **U-shaped accident curve** in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Definition of Physiological Methods (1 mark):** Methods that objectively measure the body's physical responses to work — output, accident rate, biochemical markers, cardiovascular parameters.
2. **Production Record Analysis (1 mark):** Plotting hourly output reveals a characteristic morning rise followed by an afternoon fall, indicating fatigue accumulation.
3. **Kraepelin's Work Curve (2 marks):** A graphical representation of output (Y-axis) vs. time (X-axis). The curve has three diagnostic zones: morning inertia dip, mid-shift peak, and afternoon fatigue decline. Engineering implication: schedule heavy tasks at peak hours; rest breaks must precede the steep decline zone.
4. **U-Shaped Accident Curve (2 marks):** Plotting accident frequency across shift hours reveals a U-curve. High accidents at start (inertia) and at end (fatigue), with minimum at mid-shift. This is direct evidence of fatigue-induced safety compromise.
5. **Heart Rate and Recovery Studies (1 mark):** A fatigued worker shows elevated resting HR and delayed post-task recovery (HRR Index becomes negative).

**Q A(b).** A factory worker produces **140 units in the first half** and **98 units in the second half** of an 8-hour shift. The maximum possible output under ideal rested conditions is **175 units per half-shift**. Compute the **Fatigue Index (Type-2)** and the **Productive Efficiency**. Comment on the worker's fatigue level. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark):**
- $O_1 = 140$ units
- $O_2 = 98$ units
- $O_{\text{actual, total}} = 140 + 98 = 238$ units
- $O_{\text{ideal, total}} = 175 \times 2 = 350$ units

**Step 2 — Compute Type-2 Fatigue Index (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FI &= \frac{O_1 - O_2}{O_1} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{140 - 98}{140} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{42}{140} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 30.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Productive Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{O_{\text{actual, total}}}{O_{\text{ideal, total}}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
     &= \frac{238}{350} \times 100 \\[6pt]
     &= 68.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion & Recommendation (2 marks):**
- The Fatigue Index of **30%** exceeds the 25% threshold, indicating **significant industrial fatigue**.
- Productive efficiency of only **68%** implies a 32% potential loss in throughput.
- **Recommendations:** (i) Introduce a 15-minute rest break at the 2-hour mark. (ii) Audit workstation ergonomics. (iii) Verify ambient temperature < 28°C and noise < 85 dB(A) per the Factories Act.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q B(a).** Explain **psychological methods** of studying industrial fatigue. Describe the **cancellation test (dotting test)** and the **boredom test** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Psychological methods assess the cognitive and mental dimension of fatigue, complementing the physiological measures.
2. **Cancellation Test (Dotting Test) (3 marks):** The subject is given a sheet containing rows of similar symbols (dots, letters, digits) and asked to mark/cancel a target symbol as fast and accurately as possible within a fixed time (typically 2 minutes). The total count cancelled and the error rate are recorded. **Under fatigue, both the count decreases and the error rate increases.** The test is sensitive to **visual, attention, and mental fatigue**.
3. **Boredom Test (2 marks):** The subject performs an extremely repetitive, monotonous task (e.g., sorting identical screws) for a long duration. The output curve typically shows a **steep decline after an initial peak**, demonstrating monotony-induced fatigue even when the physical load is light.
4. **Other Psychological Tests (1 mark):** Association test (word recall), Flicker fusion test (visual perception threshold), Mood scales (e.g., Samn-Perelli).

**Q B(b).** A worker produces **95 units in 2 hours** before a rest break. After a **30-minute rest**, the worker produces **80 units in the next 2 hours**. The maximum sustained output for this operator is **100 units per 2-hour block**. Compute the **Recovery Rate (RR)** and the **Fatigue Index (Type-1)**. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify values (1 mark):**
- $O_R = 80$ units (post-rest)
- $O_{\max} = 100$ units
- $O_{\text{actual, pre-rest}} = 95$ units

**Step 2 — Compute Recovery Rate (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RR &= \frac{O_R}{O_{\max}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{80}{100} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 80.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Type-1 Fatigue Index (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FI_1 &= \frac{O_{\max} - O_{\text{actual, pre-rest}}}{O_{\max}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
     &= \frac{100 - 95}{100} \times 100 \\[6pt]
     &= 5.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
- $RR = 80\%$ means the rest was **partially effective** (good recovery, but not full).
- $FI_1 = 5\%$ (based on pre-rest output) is well below the 25% threshold, suggesting the worker was **not heavily fatigued** at the time of the break — yet still didn't fully recover, possibly due to **inadequate rest duration**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Lab Reports on Industrial Fatigue:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently write $FI = 30$ instead of $FI = 30\,\%$. Always include the **% sign**. **(-0.5 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing Type-1 and Type-2 FI:** Type-1 uses $O_{\max}$ (ideal), Type-2 uses $O_1$ (first half of actual shift). Mixing them up = **wrong answer, full marks lost**.
> 3. **Skipping the "Recommendation" Step:** KTU evaluators allocate **2 of the 7 marks** to the engineering interpretation and recommendation. Writing only the calculation = **lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting the Units in the Final Answer:** Every numerical answer must carry its unit. $(units/hr)$ or $(\%)$ is mandatory. **(-0.5 marks per missing unit)**
> 5. **Writing only definitions in Part B:** The 14-mark question demands **both** theoretical explanation **and** numerical problem-solving. A purely descriptive answer gets at most **7 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting the Factories Act reference:** Always cite **Section 51 (8 hrs/day) and Section 64 (OT limit)** in long answers — examiners reward statutory awareness.
